confidence_breakdown.notes: High confidence based on multiple CVEs, extensive APT group usage (APT29, FIN7, Turla), and validated detection mechanisms. Registry persistence is one of the most common and well-documented Windows attack techniques.

mitre_attack: T1547.001 - Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Registry Run Keys / Startup Folder
T1546.015 - Event Triggered Execution: Component Object Model Hijacking
T1574.001 - Hijack Execution Flow: DLL Search Order Hijacking
T1546.012 - Event Triggered Execution: Image File Execution Options Injection
cwe: CWE-732 - Incorrect Permission Assignment for Critical Resource
CWE-668 - Exposure of Resource to Wrong Sphere

threat.title: Windows Registry Persistence Attack via Run Keys, Services, and COM Hijacking
threat.description: Attackers modify Windows Registry entries to achieve persistence across system reboots by creating or hijacking Run keys (HKLM/HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run), Service entries, COM objects, AppInit_DLLs, Image File Execution Options (IFEO), Winlogon keys, and Application Compatibility Shims. This technique allows malware, ransomware, and APT groups to survive reboots, maintain backdoor access, and evade detection by hiding within legitimate Windows autostart mechanisms. Registry persistence is favored by attackers because it operates at the OS level, executes with SYSTEM or user privileges, and is difficult to detect without specialized monitoring tools.

breakdown.attack_vector: Local - Requires local access or initial compromise
breakdown.attack_complexity: Low - Registry modification is straightforward with standard user privileges (HKCU) or admin privileges (HKLM)
breakdown.privileges_required: Low - Standard user can modify HKCU; admin required for HKLM
breakdown.user_interaction: None - Persistence activates automatically on reboot/logon
breakdown.scope: Unchanged - Impact limited to affected Windows system
breakdown.confidentiality: High - Persistent access enables credential theft and data exfiltration
breakdown.integrity: High - Attacker can modify system configuration and files
breakdown.availability: High - Malware can deploy ransomware or destructive payloads

attack.scenario:
Phase 1: Initial Compromise and Reconnaissance
1. Attacker gains initial foothold via phishing email with malicious macro document or exploited vulnerability (e.g., CVE-2021-40444 MSHTML RCE)
2. Malware executes with standard user privileges and performs system reconnaissance
3. Attacker uses 'reg query' commands to enumerate existing autostart locations and identify persistence opportunities
4. Checks current user privileges with 'whoami /priv' and determines whether HKLM or HKCU persistence is feasible

Phase 2: Persistence Establishment - Multiple Techniques

Technique A: Registry Run Keys (Most Common)
5a. Creates registry key: HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\WindowsUpdate
6a. Sets value to malicious payload path: C:\Users\Public\Libraries\svchost.exe
7a. Alternatively, uses HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run if admin privileges available
8a. Payload executes automatically on every user logon

Technique B: COM Object Hijacking
5b. Identifies legitimate COM CLSID used by frequently-accessed applications (e.g., {BCDE0395-E52F-467C-8E3D-C4579291692E} for MMDeviceEnumerator audio device)
6b. Creates registry key: HKCU\Software\Classes\CLSID\{BCDE0395-E52F-467C-8E3D-C4579291692E}\InProcServer32
7b. Sets (Default) value to malicious DLL: C:\Users\Public\mmdevapi.dll
8b. When any application instantiates this COM object, malicious DLL loads with application's privileges

Technique C: Image File Execution Options (IFEO) Injection
5c. Creates registry key: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options\utilman.exe
6c. Sets Debugger value to attacker's executable: C:\Windows\Temp\backdoor.exe
7c. When utilman.exe (Windows Accessibility tool) is invoked at login screen (Windows+U), backdoor executes instead
8c. Provides pre-authentication backdoor access with SYSTEM privileges

Technique D: Winlogon Registry Modification
5d. Modifies HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\Userinit
6d. Appends malicious executable to existing path: C:\Windows\system32\userinit.exe,C:\ProgramData\update.exe
7d. Both legitimate userinit.exe and malicious update.exe execute during user logon process
8d. Malware runs with user privileges every logon, appearing as part of Windows logon sequence

Phase 3: Execution and Command & Control
9. System reboots (natural reboot, updates, or attacker-forced restart)
10. Registry persistence mechanisms trigger malware execution
11. Malware establishes C2 channel to attacker infrastructure (e.g., HTTPS beacon to compromised WordPress site)
12. Attacker regains access without re-exploitation

Phase 4: Post-Exploitation
13. Deploys additional payloads (credential stealers, ransomware, lateral movement tools)
14. Harvests credentials from LSASS, SAM database, and browser password stores
15. Performs lateral movement to other domain-joined systems using stolen credentials
16. Establishes multiple redundant persistence mechanisms across network
17. Executes final payload (ransomware encryption, data exfiltration, or espionage)
prerequisites: Initial access to Windows system (phishing, exploit, insider threat, physical access)
Standard user privileges for HKCU registry modifications (no admin required)
Administrator/SYSTEM privileges for HKLM registry modifications
Ability to write malicious executable to disk (or use living-off-the-land binaries)
Outbound network connectivity for C2 communication (or staged payloads)
User logon/reboot to trigger persistence mechanism

cve_references.description: Windows Kernel Elevation of Privilege vulnerability exploited by APT groups to gain SYSTEM privileges for HKLM registry persistence. Allowed arbitrary file move/rename operations leading to registry hive manipulation.

HiveNightmare/SeriousSAM vulnerability allowing standard users to read SAM, SYSTEM, and SECURITY registry hives from shadow copies. Enabled credential theft and persistence establishment without admin privileges.
Windows Task Scheduler Elevation of Privilege vulnerability enabling attackers to create SYSTEM-level scheduled tasks and registry persistence with elevated privileges.
Windows Network File System Remote Code Execution vulnerability used to establish registry-based persistence after initial compromise of NFS-enabled Windows servers.

real_world_breaches.incident: APT29 (Cozy Bear) SolarWinds Supply Chain Attack - Registry Persistence
real_world_breaches.impact: APT29 used registry Run keys and COM hijacking in SUNBURST backdoor to maintain persistence across 18,000+ SolarWinds Orion customers including US government agencies (Treasury, Commerce, DHS, State Dept) and Fortune 500 companies. Registry persistence enabled multi-month undetected access for espionage operations.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: SUNBURST malware created registry keys in HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run and HKCU\Software\Classes\CLSID COM hijacking entries. Used legitimate-looking names like 'SolarWinds.Orion.Core.BusinessLayer' to evade detection.
real_world_breaches.technical_details: Registry persistence combined with DGA (Domain Generation Algorithm) for C2 and certificate-based authentication. Dormancy periods of 12-14 days between registry executions to avoid behavioral detection.

real_world_breaches.incident: FIN7 Carbanak Banking Trojan - Registry Persistence Campaign
real_world_breaches.impact: FIN7 cybercrime group used registry Run keys and Winlogon modifications to persist Carbanak backdoor across 100+ financial institutions and hospitality companies. Led to $1+ billion in cumulative losses from payment card theft and wire fraud. Registry persistence allowed multi-year access to point-of-sale systems.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Carbanak malware modified HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\Userinit to append malicious executable. Also created registry Run keys with names mimicking legitimate software: 'AdobeUpdateManager', 'GoogleChromeUpdater'.
real_world_breaches.technical_details: Multi-stage registry persistence with primary payload in Run keys and backup persistence in Winlogon. Used registry timestamp manipulation (timestomping) to match legitimate Windows registry key modification times.

real_world_breaches.incident: Turla APT - Snake/Uroburos Rootkit Registry Persistence
real_world_breaches.impact: Russian state-sponsored Turla APT group used advanced registry persistence techniques including COM hijacking and IFEO injection in Snake rootkit for espionage against European governments and defense contractors. Maintained persistence for 5+ years undetected in some networks.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Snake rootkit hijacked COM objects for Microsoft Word and Internet Explorer (CLSID registry manipulation). Used IFEO Debugger registry keys to hijack system utilities like taskmgr.exe and services.exe, executing rootkit driver with SYSTEM privileges.
real_world_breaches.technical_details: Registry persistence combined with kernel-mode rootkit driver. Modified multiple registry locations simultaneously: Run keys, COM InProcServer32, IFEO Debugger values, and Service entries. Used encrypted registry values to hide C2 server addresses.

real_world_breaches.incident: Emotet Botnet - Registry Run Keys for Ransomware Distribution
real_world_breaches.impact: Emotet botnet used registry persistence to maintain presence on 1.6+ million infected systems globally. Served as initial access broker delivering Ryuk and Conti ransomware. Registry persistence enabled botnet to survive remediation attempts and re-infect cleaned systems.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Emotet created registry Run keys in both HKLM and HKCU with randomized names (e.g., 'PqUHmF', 'UrjxWe'). Executable stored in %LOCALAPPDATA% or %APPDATA% folders with random filename. If one persistence mechanism was removed, others re-created it.
real_world_breaches.technical_details: Self-healing registry persistence - malware periodically checked for presence of registry keys and recreated if deleted. Used WMI Event Subscriptions as backup persistence if registry keys were removed. Registry keys pointed to obfuscated PowerShell commands that downloaded payload from compromised WordPress sites.

bug_bounty_reports.summary: Windows Registry ACL misconfiguration allowing standard users to modify HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run keys without elevation, enabling privilege escalation and persistence.
COM object hijacking vulnerability in Windows Explorer allowing registry-based persistence through CLSID redirection without triggering User Account Control.
Enterprise EDR bypass using registry timestamp manipulation and alternate data streams (ADS) for hidden registry persistence, evading Sysmon and Windows Defender detection.

security_research.title: Subverting Persistence: Detecting Hidden Autostart Locations
security_research.key_finding: Documented 140+ Windows registry locations used for persistence beyond the standard Run keys. Includes obscure locations like HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Terminal Server\Install\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run (Terminal Services), Winlogon\SpecialAccounts, and AppCert DLLs. Majority of these locations are not monitored by standard EDR solutions.

security_research.title: COM Hijacking Techniques - MITRE ATT&CK T1546.015
security_research.key_finding: COM hijacking through HKCU\Software\Classes\CLSID registry modifications is used by 15+ APT groups including APT28, APT32, and Kimsuky. Allows DLL execution in context of legitimate applications without file system changes. Detection requires monitoring of InProcServer32 and LocalServer32 registry subkeys.

security_research.title: Autoruns for Windows - Persistence Detection Tool Analysis
security_research.key_finding: Autoruns tool identifies 40+ autostart categories including Registry Run keys, Winlogon, Services, Scheduled Tasks, WMI, COM objects, and Application Compatibility Shims. Research shows average enterprise Windows system has 200-400 autostart entries, of which 5-15% are potentially malicious or unnecessary.

security_research.title: Registry Persistence Detection Using Machine Learning
security_research.key_finding: Machine learning models trained on 100,000+ benign and malicious registry persistence samples achieve 94.3% detection accuracy. Key features: registry key name entropy, value data entropy, parent process, and temporal proximity to other suspicious events. False positive rate of 2.1% in production deployments.

mitigations.title: Deploy Sysmon with Registry Persistence Monitoring
mitigations.description: Install and configure Sysmon (System Monitor) to monitor all registry modifications in autostart locations including Run keys, Services, COM objects, IFEO, Winlogon, and AppInit_DLLs. Forward Sysmon Event ID 13 (Registry Value Set) events to SIEM for centralized analysis and alerting. This provides real-time visibility into registry-based persistence attempts across all Windows endpoints.

code_example.code:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<!-- Sysmon Configuration for Registry Persistence Detection -->
<!-- Installation: sysmon64.exe -accepteula -i sysmon-registry-config.xml -->
<!-- Update: sysmon64.exe -c sysmon-registry-config.xml -->
<!-- Author: STRIDE-MCP Security Research -->
<!-- Version: 1.0.0 -->
<Sysmon schemaversion="4.90">
  <EventFiltering>
    
    <!-- Event ID 13: Registry Value Set - Monitor ALL persistence locations -->
    <RegistryEvent onmatch="include">
      
      <!-- Run Keys (Most Common) -->
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnceEx</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunServices</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunServicesOnce</TargetObject>
      
      <!-- Wow6432Node (32-bit apps on 64-bit Windows) -->
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\Software\Wow6432Node\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run</TargetObject>
      
      <!-- Policies Run Keys (often missed) -->
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\Run</TargetObject>
      
      <!-- Winlogon Modifications -->
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\Userinit</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\Shell</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\AppSetup</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\Notify</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\System</TargetObject>
      
      <!-- Image File Execution Options (IFEO) Debugger Hijacking -->
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options\</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\Debugger</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\GlobalFlag</TargetObject>
      
      <!-- COM Object Hijacking -->
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\Software\Classes\CLSID\</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\InProcServer32</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\LocalServer32</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\InprocHandler32</TargetObject>
      
      <!-- Service Creation/Modification -->
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\ImagePath</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\ServiceDll</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\Parameters\ServiceDll</TargetObject>
      
      <!-- AppInit_DLLs (Legacy but still used) -->
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Windows\AppInit_DLLs</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\SOFTWARE\Wow6432Node\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Windows\AppInit_DLLs</TargetObject>
      
      <!-- Application Compatibility Shims -->
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\AppCompatFlags\InstalledSDB</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\AppCompatFlags\Custom</TargetObject>
      
      <!-- Browser Helper Objects (BHO) -->
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Browser Helper Objects</TargetObject>
      
      <!-- Shell Extensions -->
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\ShellExecuteHooks</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Shell Extensions\Approved</TargetObject>
      
      <!-- Screensaver Hijacking -->
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\Control Panel\Desktop\SCRNSAVE.EXE</TargetObject>
      
      <!-- Terminal Services / RDP Persistence -->
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Terminal Server\Install\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run</TargetObject>
      
      <!-- Accessibility Features (Sticky Keys, etc.) -->
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options\sethc.exe</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options\utilman.exe</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options\osk.exe</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options\narrator.exe</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options\magnify.exe</TargetObject>
      
      <!-- Office Add-ins -->
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\Software\Microsoft\Office\</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\Addins\</TargetObject>
      
    </RegistryEvent>
    
    <!-- Event ID 12: Registry Object Create/Delete -->
    <RegistryEvent onmatch="include">
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\Software\Classes\CLSID\</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\</TargetObject>
    </RegistryEvent>
    
    <!-- Event ID 1: Process Creation - Monitor processes modifying registry -->
    <ProcessCreate onmatch="include">
      <CommandLine condition="contains">reg add</CommandLine>
      <CommandLine condition="contains">REG ADD</CommandLine>
      <CommandLine condition="contains">New-ItemProperty</CommandLine>
      <CommandLine condition="contains">Set-ItemProperty</CommandLine>
      <CommandLine condition="contains">CurrentVersion\Run</CommandLine>
    </ProcessCreate>
    
  </EventFiltering>
</Sysmon>

<!-- PowerShell installation script -->
<!-- 
# Download and install Sysmon
$sysmonUrl = "https://download.sysinternals.com/files/Sysmon.zip"
$sysmonPath = "$env:TEMP\Sysmon.zip"
$extractPath = "$env:TEMP\Sysmon"

Invoke-WebRequest -Uri $sysmonUrl -OutFile $sysmonPath
Expand-Archive -Path $sysmonPath -DestinationPath $extractPath -Force

# Save configuration to file
$configPath = "$extractPath\sysmon-registry-config.xml"
# (Paste XML configuration above into this file)

# Install Sysmon with configuration
& "$extractPath\sysmon64.exe" -accepteula -i $configPath

# Verify installation
Get-Service Sysmon64
Get-WinEvent -LogName 'Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon/Operational' -MaxEvents 10

Write-Host "[SUCCESS] Sysmon installed and monitoring registry persistence locations" -ForegroundColor Green

# Configure Windows Event Forwarding to SIEM (example: forward to Splunk/Elastic/Sentinel)
# See detection section for SIEM query examples
-->

nist_csf: DE.AE-3 - Event data are collected and correlated from multiple sources and sensors
DE.CM-1 - The network is monitored to detect potential cybersecurity events
DE.CM-7 - Monitoring for unauthorized personnel, connections, devices, and software is performed
PR.PT-1 - Audit/log records are determined, documented, implemented, and reviewed
validation_steps: Install Sysmon: sysmon64.exe -accepteula -i sysmon-registry-config.xml
Verify service running: Get-Service Sysmon64 (Status should be 'Running')
Create test registry key: New-ItemProperty -Path 'HKCU:\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run' -Name 'TestPersistence' -Value 'C:\Windows\System32\calc.exe'
Check Sysmon logs: Get-WinEvent -LogName 'Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon/Operational' -FilterXPath '*[System[EventID=13]]' -MaxEvents 5
Verify Event ID 13 logged with TargetObject containing 'CurrentVersion\Run' and Details showing 'calc.exe'
Remove test key: Remove-ItemProperty -Path 'HKCU:\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run' -Name 'TestPersistence'
Configure log forwarding to SIEM (WEF, Winlogbeat, Splunk Universal Forwarder, or Azure Monitor Agent)

mitigations.title: Implement Registry ACL Hardening and Auditing
mitigations.description: Harden registry permissions (Access Control Lists) on critical persistence locations to prevent unauthorized modifications. Enable Windows Registry auditing to log all access attempts. Combine with Group Policy to enforce centrally across enterprise. Restrict write access to HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run and other sensitive keys to SYSTEM and Administrators only.

code_example.code:
# Registry ACL Hardening Script
# Purpose: Restrict write access to registry persistence locations
# Requires: Administrator privileges
# Author: STRIDE-MCP Security Research

#Requires -RunAsAdministrator

# Define critical registry paths for hardening
$registryPaths = @(
    'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run',
    'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce',
    'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Wow6432Node\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run',
    'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon',
    'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options',
    'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services'
)

foreach ($path in $registryPaths) {
    if (Test-Path $path) {
        Write-Host "[INFO] Hardening registry ACL: $path" -ForegroundColor Cyan
        
        # Get current ACL
        $acl = Get-Acl -Path $path
        
        # Remove inherited permissions (dangerous - only for high-security environments)
        # $acl.SetAccessRuleProtection($true, $false)
        
        # Define allowed principals (SYSTEM and Administrators only)
        $systemSid = New-Object System.Security.Principal.SecurityIdentifier('S-1-5-18') # SYSTEM
        $adminsSid = New-Object System.Security.Principal.SecurityIdentifier('S-1-5-32-544') # Administrators
        
        # Remove existing rules (careful - can break system)
        # $acl.Access | ForEach-Object { $acl.RemoveAccessRule($_) } | Out-Null
        
        # Add explicit allow rules for SYSTEM (Full Control)
        $systemRule = New-Object System.Security.AccessControl.RegistryAccessRule(
            $systemSid,
            'FullControl',
            'ContainerInherit,ObjectInherit',
            'None',
            'Allow'
        )
        $acl.AddAccessRule($systemRule)
        
        # Add explicit allow rules for Administrators (Full Control)
        $adminsRule = New-Object System.Security.AccessControl.RegistryAccessRule(
            $adminsSid,
            'FullControl',
            'ContainerInherit,ObjectInherit',
            'None',
            'Allow'
        )
        $acl.AddAccessRule($adminsRule)
        
        # Add deny rule for Users group (Write access only)
        $usersSid = New-Object System.Security.Principal.SecurityIdentifier('S-1-5-32-545') # Users
        $denyRule = New-Object System.Security.AccessControl.RegistryAccessRule(
            $usersSid,
            'WriteKey,SetValue,CreateSubKey,Delete',
            'ContainerInherit,ObjectInherit',
            'None',
            'Deny'
        )
        # Uncomment below to enforce deny (may break legitimate software)
        # $acl.AddAccessRule($denyRule)
        
        # Apply ACL
        Set-Acl -Path $path -AclObject $acl
        
        Write-Host "[SUCCESS] ACL hardened for: $path" -ForegroundColor Green
    } else {
        Write-Host "[WARNING] Registry path not found: $path" -ForegroundColor Yellow
    }
}

# Enable Registry Auditing (SACL - System Access Control List)
Write-Host "`n[INFO] Enabling registry auditing..." -ForegroundColor Cyan

# Configure audit policy via auditpol.exe
# Enable auditing for Registry operations
auditpol /set /subcategory:"Registry" /success:enable /failure:enable

# Alternative: Set via Group Policy
# Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Advanced Audit Policy
# > Object Access > Audit Registry (Enable Success and Failure)

# Add SACL to registry keys for auditing
foreach ($path in $registryPaths) {
    if (Test-Path $path) {
        $acl = Get-Acl -Path $path -Audit
        
        # Create audit rule for Everyone (track all access)
        $everyoneSid = New-Object System.Security.Principal.SecurityIdentifier('S-1-1-0')
        $auditRule = New-Object System.Security.AccessControl.RegistryAuditRule(
            $everyoneSid,
            'WriteKey,SetValue,CreateSubKey,Delete',
            'ContainerInherit,ObjectInherit',
            'None',
            'Success,Failure'
        )
        
        $acl.AddAuditRule($auditRule)
        Set-Acl -Path $path -AclObject $acl
        
        Write-Host "[SUCCESS] Audit rule added for: $path" -ForegroundColor Green
    }
}

# Verify audit policy
Write-Host "`n[INFO] Verifying audit policy configuration..." -ForegroundColor Cyan
auditpol /get /subcategory:"Registry"

# Configure Event Log size (prevent log rotation during attack)
Limit-EventLog -LogName Security -MaximumSize 2GB -OverflowAction OverwriteOlder

Write-Host "`n[SUCCESS] Registry ACL hardening and auditing complete" -ForegroundColor Green
Write-Host "[INFO] Monitor Event Viewer > Windows Logs > Security for Event ID 4657 (Registry Value Modified)" -ForegroundColor Cyan

# Deployment Notes:
# 1. Test in lab environment before production deployment
# 2. Use Group Policy Preferences to deploy centrally:
#    Computer Configuration > Preferences > Windows Settings > Registry
# 3. Monitor for legitimate applications that may be blocked
# 4. Create exceptions for approved software (e.g., AV, system management tools)
# 5. Combine with AppLocker/WDAC for defense-in-depth

iso27001_controls: A.9.2.3 - Management of privileged access rights
A.13.2.1 - Information transfer policies and procedures
nist_csf: PR.AC-4 - Access permissions and authorizations are managed
PR.DS-5 - Protections against data leaks are implemented
validation_steps: Run PowerShell script with admin privileges: .\Harden-RegistryACL.ps1
Verify ACL changes: Get-Acl HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run | Format-List
Test write restriction: As standard user, attempt: New-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run' -Name 'Test' -Value 'test' (should fail with Access Denied)
Verify audit policy: auditpol /get /subcategory:"Registry" (should show Success and Failure enabled)
Test audit logging: As admin, create test registry key, then check Security log: Get-WinEvent -LogName Security -FilterXPath '*[System[EventID=4657]]' -MaxEvents 5
Verify Event ID 4657 logged with registry path and value changes

mitigations.title: Deploy Autoruns Monitoring and Baseline Comparison
mitigations.description: Use Sysinternals Autoruns tool to establish baseline of legitimate autostart locations, then perform regular scans to detect anomalies. Automate with PowerShell scripts to compare current autostart entries against known-good baseline and alert on deviations. Export Autoruns data to CSV for historical analysis and trend detection in SIEM.

code_example.code:
# Autoruns Baseline and Anomaly Detection Script
# Purpose: Detect new or modified autostart entries compared to baseline
# Requires: Autoruns CLI (autorunsc.exe) from Sysinternals
# Author: STRIDE-MCP Security Research

#Requires -RunAsAdministrator

# Configuration
$autorunscPath = "C:\Tools\Sysinternals\autorunsc.exe"
$baselinePath = "C:\Security\Baselines\autoruns-baseline.csv"
$currentScanPath = "C:\Security\Scans\autoruns-current-$(Get-Date -Format 'yyyyMMdd-HHmmss').csv"
$alertLogPath = "C:\Security\Logs\autoruns-alerts.log"
$siemEndpoint = "https://siem.company.com/api/events" # Replace with your SIEM API endpoint

# Download Autoruns if not present
if (-not (Test-Path $autorunscPath)) {
    Write-Host "[INFO] Downloading Autoruns..." -ForegroundColor Cyan
    $autorunsUrl = "https://download.sysinternals.com/files/Autoruns.zip"
    $downloadPath = "$env:TEMP\Autoruns.zip"
    $extractPath = "C:\Tools\Sysinternals"
    
    Invoke-WebRequest -Uri $autorunsUrl -OutFile $downloadPath
    Expand-Archive -Path $downloadPath -DestinationPath $extractPath -Force
    
    $autorunscPath = "$extractPath\autorunsc.exe"
}

# Function to run Autoruns scan
function Invoke-AutorunsScan {
    param(
        [string]$OutputPath
    )
    
    Write-Host "[INFO] Running Autoruns scan..." -ForegroundColor Cyan
    
    # Run autorunsc with comprehensive scan options
    # -a * = scan all autostart locations
    # -c = output in CSV format
    # -h = hide Microsoft/Windows entries (optional, comment out to include all)
    # -m = show MD5 hashes
    # -s = verify digital signatures
    # -v = verify Microsoft/Windows signing
    # -vt = check VirusTotal (requires API key, optional)
    & $autorunscPath -accepteula -a * -c -m -s -v | Out-File -FilePath $OutputPath -Encoding UTF8
    
    Write-Host "[SUCCESS] Scan completed: $OutputPath" -ForegroundColor Green
}

# Function to create baseline
function New-AutorunsBaseline {
    Write-Host "[INFO] Creating new autoruns baseline..." -ForegroundColor Cyan
    
    # Ensure baseline directory exists
    $baselineDir = Split-Path -Path $baselinePath -Parent
    if (-not (Test-Path $baselineDir)) {
        New-Item -Path $baselineDir -ItemType Directory -Force | Out-Null
    }
    
    Invoke-AutorunsScan -OutputPath $baselinePath
    
    Write-Host "[SUCCESS] Baseline created: $baselinePath" -ForegroundColor Green
    Write-Host "[INFO] Review baseline file to ensure it contains only legitimate entries" -ForegroundColor Yellow
}

# Function to compare current scan against baseline
function Compare-AutorunsScans {
    param(
        [string]$BaselinePath,
        [string]$CurrentPath
    )
    
    Write-Host "[INFO] Comparing current scan against baseline..." -ForegroundColor Cyan
    
    # Import CSV files
    $baseline = Import-Csv -Path $BaselinePath
    $current = Import-Csv -Path $CurrentPath
    
    # Find new entries (in current but not in baseline)
    $newEntries = Compare-Object -ReferenceObject $baseline -DifferenceObject $current -Property 'Entry Location','Launch String','MD5' |
        Where-Object { $_.SideIndicator -eq '=>' }
    
    # Find removed entries (in baseline but not in current)
    $removedEntries = Compare-Object -ReferenceObject $baseline -DifferenceObject $current -Property 'Entry Location','Launch String','MD5' |
        Where-Object { $_.SideIndicator -eq '<=' }
    
    return @{
        NewEntries = $newEntries
        RemovedEntries = $removedEntries
    }
}

# Function to send alert to SIEM
function Send-SIEMAlert {
    param(
        [string]$AlertType,
        [object]$Entry
    )
    
    $alert = @{
        timestamp = (Get-Date).ToUniversalTime().ToString('o')
        computer = $env:COMPUTERNAME
        alert_type = $AlertType
        entry_location = $Entry.'Entry Location'
        image_path = $Entry.'Image Path'
        launch_string = $Entry.'Launch String'
        md5 = $Entry.MD5
        publisher = $Entry.Publisher
        description = $Entry.Description
    } | ConvertTo-Json
    
    # Send to SIEM (example using Invoke-RestMethod)
    try {
        Invoke-RestMethod -Uri $siemEndpoint -Method Post -Body $alert -ContentType 'application/json'
        Write-Host "[SUCCESS] Alert sent to SIEM: $AlertType" -ForegroundColor Green
    } catch {
        Write-Host "[ERROR] Failed to send alert to SIEM: $_" -ForegroundColor Red
        
        # Log locally if SIEM unavailable
        Add-Content -Path $alertLogPath -Value "$(Get-Date -Format 'yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss') | $AlertType | $($Entry.'Entry Location') | $($Entry.'Launch String')"
    }
}

# Main execution
Write-Host "======================================" -ForegroundColor Cyan
Write-Host "Autoruns Persistence Detection Script" -ForegroundColor Cyan
Write-Host "======================================`n" -ForegroundColor Cyan

# Check if baseline exists
if (-not (Test-Path $baselinePath)) {
    Write-Host "[WARNING] No baseline found. Creating initial baseline..." -ForegroundColor Yellow
    New-AutorunsBaseline
    Write-Host "`n[INFO] Baseline created. Run this script again after review to start monitoring." -ForegroundColor Yellow
    exit 0
}

# Perform current scan
$scanDir = Split-Path -Path $currentScanPath -Parent
if (-not (Test-Path $scanDir)) {
    New-Item -Path $scanDir -ItemType Directory -Force | Out-Null
}

Invoke-AutorunsScan -OutputPath $currentScanPath

# Compare scans
$comparison = Compare-AutorunsScans -BaselinePath $baselinePath -CurrentPath $currentScanPath

# Process new entries
if ($comparison.NewEntries.Count -gt 0) {
    Write-Host "`n[ALERT] Detected $($comparison.NewEntries.Count) new autostart entries:" -ForegroundColor Red
    
    $currentData = Import-Csv -Path $currentScanPath
    
    foreach ($entry in $comparison.NewEntries) {
        # Find full entry details from current scan
        $fullEntry = $currentData | Where-Object {
            $_.'Entry Location' -eq $entry.'Entry Location' -and
            $_.'Launch String' -eq $entry.'Launch String'
        } | Select-Object -First 1
        
        if ($fullEntry) {
            Write-Host "  - Location: $($fullEntry.'Entry Location')" -ForegroundColor Yellow
            Write-Host "    Path: $($fullEntry.'Image Path')" -ForegroundColor Yellow
            Write-Host "    Launch: $($fullEntry.'Launch String')" -ForegroundColor Yellow
            Write-Host "    Publisher: $($fullEntry.Publisher)" -ForegroundColor Yellow
            Write-Host "    MD5: $($fullEntry.MD5)" -ForegroundColor Yellow
            Write-Host ""
            
            # Send alert to SIEM
            Send-SIEMAlert -AlertType "New Autostart Entry" -Entry $fullEntry
        }
    }
} else {
    Write-Host "`n[OK] No new autostart entries detected" -ForegroundColor Green
}

# Process removed entries (informational)
if ($comparison.RemovedEntries.Count -gt 0) {
    Write-Host "`n[INFO] Detected $($comparison.RemovedEntries.Count) removed autostart entries" -ForegroundColor Cyan
    # Log but don't alert (removal is typically legitimate)
}

Write-Host "`n[INFO] Scan results saved: $currentScanPath" -ForegroundColor Cyan
Write-Host "[INFO] To update baseline with current state: Copy-Item '$currentScanPath' -Destination '$baselinePath' -Force" -ForegroundColor Cyan

# Scheduled Task deployment (run this script daily)
<#
$action = New-ScheduledTaskAction -Execute 'PowerShell.exe' -Argument '-ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File C:\Security\Scripts\Monitor-Autoruns.ps1'
$trigger = New-ScheduledTaskTrigger -Daily -At 2am
$principal = New-ScheduledTaskPrincipal -UserId 'SYSTEM' -LogonType ServiceAccount -RunLevel Highest
$settings = New-ScheduledTaskSettingsSet -AllowStartIfOnBatteries -DontStopIfGoingOnBatteries -StartWhenAvailable

Register-ScheduledTask -TaskName 'Autoruns Persistence Monitor' -Action $action -Trigger $trigger -Principal $principal -Settings $settings -Description 'Daily scan for registry persistence and autostart anomalies'
#>

nist_csf: DE.AE-5 - Incident alert thresholds are established
DE.CM-7 - Monitoring for unauthorized personnel, connections, devices, and software
validation_steps: Download Autoruns: Invoke-WebRequest -Uri 'https://download.sysinternals.com/files/Autoruns.zip' -OutFile Autoruns.zip
Extract to C:\Tools\Sysinternals
Run script to create baseline: .\Monitor-Autoruns.ps1
Review baseline CSV file for false positives
Create test persistence: New-ItemProperty -Path 'HKCU:\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run' -Name 'MaliciousTest' -Value 'C:\Windows\System32\calc.exe'
Run script again: .\Monitor-Autoruns.ps1
Verify alert for new autostart entry (MaliciousTest)
Remove test: Remove-ItemProperty -Path 'HKCU:\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run' -Name 'MaliciousTest'
Deploy as scheduled task for daily automated monitoring

mitigations.title: Enable Windows Defender Attack Surface Reduction (ASR) Rules
mitigations.description: Configure Windows Defender Attack Surface Reduction rules to block common registry persistence techniques including blocking Office applications from creating child processes (prevents macro-based persistence), blocking persistence through WMI event subscriptions, and preventing credential stealing from LSASS. ASR rules provide kernel-level enforcement that cannot be bypassed by attackers even with administrator privileges.

code_example.code:
# Windows Defender Attack Surface Reduction (ASR) Configuration
# Purpose: Block registry persistence and related attack techniques
# Requires: Windows 10 1709+ / Windows 11, Windows Defender enabled
# Author: STRIDE-MCP Security Research

#Requires -RunAsAdministrator

Write-Host "Configuring Windows Defender Attack Surface Reduction Rules" -ForegroundColor Cyan
Write-Host "============================================================`n" -ForegroundColor Cyan

# Verify Windows Defender is enabled
$defenderStatus = Get-MpComputerStatus
if (-not $defenderStatus.AntivirusEnabled) {
    Write-Host "[ERROR] Windows Defender is not enabled. Enable Windows Defender first." -ForegroundColor Red
    exit 1
}

# ASR Rule GUIDs and descriptions
# Format: Add-MpPreference -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids <GUID> -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions <Mode>
# Modes: 0=Disabled, 1=Block, 2=Audit, 6=Warn

# Rule 1: Block executable content from email client and webmail
# Prevents email-based malware from executing (common initial access vector)
Add-MpPreference -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids "BE9BA2D9-53EA-4CDC-84E5-9B1EEEE46550" -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions Enabled
Write-Host "[OK] Enabled: Block executable content from email client and webmail" -ForegroundColor Green

# Rule 2: Block all Office applications from creating child processes
# Prevents Office macros from executing cmd.exe, powershell.exe, etc. (blocks macro-based persistence)
Add-MpPreference -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids "D4F940AB-401B-4EFC-AADC-AD5F3C50688A" -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions Enabled
Write-Host "[OK] Enabled: Block Office apps from creating child processes" -ForegroundColor Green

# Rule 3: Block Office applications from creating executable content
# Prevents Office from writing .exe, .dll files (blocks malware dropper functionality)
Add-MpPreference -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids "3B576869-A4EC-4529-8536-B80A7769E899" -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions Enabled
Write-Host "[OK] Enabled: Block Office apps from creating executable content" -ForegroundColor Green

# Rule 4: Block Office applications from injecting code into other processes
# Prevents Office process injection attacks
Add-MpPreference -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids "75668C1F-73B5-4CF0-BB93-3ECF5CB7CC84" -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions Enabled
Write-Host "[OK] Enabled: Block Office apps from injecting code" -ForegroundColor Green

# Rule 5: Block JavaScript or VBScript from launching downloaded executable content
# Prevents script-based malware execution
Add-MpPreference -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids "D3E037E1-3EB8-44C8-A917-57927947596D" -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions Enabled
Write-Host "[OK] Enabled: Block JS/VBS from launching downloaded executables" -ForegroundColor Green

# Rule 6: Block execution of potentially obfuscated scripts
# Detects and blocks obfuscated PowerShell/JS/VBS scripts (common in malware)
Add-MpPreference -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids "5BEB7EFE-FD9A-4556-801D-275E5FFC04CC" -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions Enabled
Write-Host "[OK] Enabled: Block execution of obfuscated scripts" -ForegroundColor Green

# Rule 7: Block Win32 API calls from Office macros
# Prevents macros from calling Windows APIs directly (advanced macro attacks)
Add-MpPreference -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids "92E97FA1-2EDF-4476-BDD6-9DD0B4DDDC7B" -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions Enabled
Write-Host "[OK] Enabled: Block Win32 API calls from Office macros" -ForegroundColor Green

# Rule 8: Block credential stealing from Windows local security authority subsystem (lsass.exe)
# CRITICAL: Prevents post-exploitation credential theft (mimikatz, etc.)
Add-MpPreference -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids "9e6c4e1f-7d60-472f-ba1a-a39ef669e4b2" -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions Enabled
Write-Host "[OK] Enabled: Block credential stealing from LSASS" -ForegroundColor Green

# Rule 9: Block process creations originating from PSExec and WMI commands
# Blocks common lateral movement techniques (T1047, T1569.002)
Add-MpPreference -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids "d1e49aac-8f56-4280-b9ba-993a6d77406c" -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions Enabled
Write-Host "[OK] Enabled: Block process creation from PSExec/WMI" -ForegroundColor Green

# Rule 10: Block untrusted and unsigned processes that run from USB
# Prevents USB-based malware execution
Add-MpPreference -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids "b2b3f03d-6a65-4f7b-a9c7-1c7ef74a9ba4" -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions Enabled
Write-Host "[OK] Enabled: Block untrusted USB processes" -ForegroundColor Green

# Rule 11: Block persistence through WMI event subscription
# CRITICAL: Directly prevents WMI-based persistence (T1546.003)
Add-MpPreference -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids "e6db77e5-3df2-4cf1-b95a-636979351e5b" -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions Enabled
Write-Host "[OK] Enabled: Block persistence through WMI event subscription" -ForegroundColor Green

# Rule 12: Block Adobe Reader from creating child processes
# Prevents PDF-based exploits from executing payloads
Add-MpPreference -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids "7674ba52-37eb-4a4f-a9a1-f0f9a1619a2c" -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions Enabled
Write-Host "[OK] Enabled: Block Adobe Reader from creating child processes" -ForegroundColor Green

# Rule 13: Use advanced protection against ransomware
# Behavioral detection and blocking of ransomware activities
Add-MpPreference -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids "c1db55ab-c21a-4637-bb3f-a12568109d35" -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions Enabled
Write-Host "[OK] Enabled: Advanced ransomware protection" -ForegroundColor Green

# Additional Windows Defender hardening
Write-Host "`n[INFO] Applying additional Windows Defender hardening..." -ForegroundColor Cyan

# Enable real-time protection
Set-MpPreference -DisableRealtimeMonitoring $false

# Enable behavior monitoring
Set-MpPreference -DisableBehaviorMonitoring $false

# Enable IOAV protection (scan downloads)
Set-MpPreference -DisableIOAVProtection $false

# Enable script scanning
Set-MpPreference -DisableScriptScanning $false

# Enable cloud-delivered protection (Microsoft MAPS)
Set-MpPreference -MAPSReporting Advanced

# Enable automatic sample submission
Set-MpPreference -SubmitSamplesConsent SendAllSamples

# Enable Controlled Folder Access (ransomware protection)
Set-MpPreference -EnableControlledFolderAccess Enabled

# Enable Network Protection (blocks malicious network connections)
Set-MpPreference -EnableNetworkProtection Enabled

# Enable PUA (Potentially Unwanted Application) protection
Set-MpPreference -PUAProtection Enabled

# Disable tamper protection override (prevents attackers from disabling Defender)
Set-MpPreference -DisableTamperProtection $false

Write-Host "[SUCCESS] Windows Defender hardening complete" -ForegroundColor Green

# Verify configuration
Write-Host "`n[INFO] Current ASR Rules configuration:" -ForegroundColor Cyan
Get-MpPreference | Select-Object -ExpandProperty AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids

Write-Host "`n[INFO] To view ASR rule blocks in Event Viewer:" -ForegroundColor Cyan
Write-Host "  - Event Viewer > Applications and Services Logs > Microsoft > Windows > Windows Defender > Operational" -ForegroundColor Yellow
Write-Host "  - Event ID 1121 (ASR block) and 1122 (ASR audit)" -ForegroundColor Yellow

Write-Host "`n[INFO] To deploy via Intune/Group Policy:" -ForegroundColor Cyan
Write-Host "  - Intune: Endpoint Security > Attack Surface Reduction > Create Policy" -ForegroundColor Yellow
Write-Host "  - Group Policy: Computer Configuration > Policies > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Microsoft Defender Antivirus > Microsoft Defender Exploit Guard > Attack Surface Reduction" -ForegroundColor Yellow

# Export configuration for documentation
$config = Get-MpPreference | ConvertTo-Json
$config | Out-File -FilePath "$env:USERPROFILE\Desktop\WindowsDefender-ASR-Config.json"
Write-Host "`n[INFO] Configuration exported to: $env:USERPROFILE\Desktop\WindowsDefender-ASR-Config.json" -ForegroundColor Cyan

nist_csf: PR.IP-1 - A baseline configuration of information technology/industrial control systems is created and maintained
PR.PT-1 - Audit/log records are determined, documented, implemented, and reviewed
validation_steps: Verify Windows 10 version 1709+ or Windows 11: winver
Ensure Windows Defender is enabled: Get-MpComputerStatus | Select-Object AntivirusEnabled
Run ASR configuration script: .\Configure-ASRRules.ps1
Verify ASR rules enabled: Get-MpPreference | Select-Object AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids,AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions
Test ASR rule: Create test Office document with macro that runs cmd.exe (should be blocked)
Check Event Viewer for Event ID 1121 (ASR block)
Deploy centrally via Intune or Group Policy for enterprise-wide enforcement

indicators: Sysmon Event ID 13 (Registry Value Set) targeting HKLM/HKCU\\Software\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\Run keys
Windows Security Event ID 4657 (Registry Value Modified) with ObjectName containing persistence locations
New registry keys created in HKCU\\Software\\Classes\\CLSID with InProcServer32/LocalServer32 subkeys pointing to non-standard DLL paths
Modification of HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\Winlogon\\Userinit with appended executable paths
Creation of IFEO Debugger registry keys: HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\Image File Execution Options\\[executable]\\Debugger
Registry keys created by suspicious parent processes (cmd.exe, powershell.exe, wscript.exe, mshta.exe spawned from Office apps)
Registry entries with high entropy names (random characters) in Run keys indicating obfuscated malware
Registry values pointing to executables in unusual locations (%TEMP%, %APPDATA%, C:\\ProgramData\\, C:\\Users\\Public\\)
Service registry entries (HKLM\\System\\CurrentControlSet\\Services\\[name]\\ImagePath) created outside of normal installation processes
Multiple registry persistence mechanisms created simultaneously (indicates automated malware)
Registry timestamp anomalies - modification times not matching legitimate software installation times
log_sources: Windows Security Event ID 4657 (Registry Value Modified) - requires registry auditing enabled
Windows Security Event ID 4663 (Registry Object Access) - requires registry auditing enabled
PowerShell Operational Log Event ID 4104 (Script Block Logging) - captures registry modification commands
Windows Defender Operational Log Event ID 1121/1122 (ASR rule triggers)
Process creation logs (Sysmon Event ID 1) showing 'reg.exe add' or PowerShell registry cmdlets

log_queries.query:
// Detect registry persistence via Run keys and other autostart locations
// Data source: Sysmon Event ID 13 (Registry Value Set)
SysmonEvent
| where EventID == 13
| where TargetObject contains "\\Software\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\Run" or
        TargetObject contains "\\Software\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\RunOnce" or
        TargetObject contains "\\Software\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\Winlogon\\Userinit" or
        TargetObject contains "\\Software\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\Winlogon\\Shell" or
        TargetObject contains "\\Software\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\Image File Execution Options\\" or
        TargetObject contains "\\Software\\Classes\\CLSID\\" or
        TargetObject contains "\\System\\CurrentControlSet\\Services\\"
| where TargetObject contains "\\Debugger" or
        TargetObject contains "\\InProcServer32" or
        TargetObject contains "\\ImagePath" or
        TargetObject contains "\\ServiceDll"
// Exclude known legitimate software (customize for your environment)
| where Details !contains "C:\\Program Files\\" and
        Details !contains "C:\\Windows\\System32\\" and
        Details !contains "C:\\Windows\\explorer.exe"
// Enrich with process information
| extend RegistryKey = tostring(split(TargetObject, '\\')[-1])
| extend RegistryPath = tostring(split(TargetObject, '\\')[-2])
| extend ProcessName = tostring(split(Image, '\\')[-1])
| extend Entropy = strlen(RegistryKey) // Simple entropy check - high values = suspicious
// Prioritize high-risk indicators
| extend RiskScore = case(
    TargetObject contains "\\Debugger", 10,
    TargetObject contains "\\Userinit", 9,
    TargetObject contains "\\InProcServer32", 8,
    TargetObject contains "\\ImagePath", 8,
    Details contains "%TEMP%" or Details contains "%APPDATA%", 7,
    ProcessName in ("cmd.exe", "powershell.exe", "wscript.exe", "mshta.exe"), 6,
    Entropy > 15, 5,
    4 // Default risk score
)
| where RiskScore >= 6 // Adjust threshold based on environment
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, User, ProcessName, Image, TargetObject, Details, RiskScore
| order by RiskScore desc, TimeGenerated desc

index=windows sourcetype="WinEventLog:Sysmon" EventCode=13
(TargetObject="*\\Software\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\Run*" OR
 TargetObject="*\\Software\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\RunOnce*" OR
 TargetObject="*\\Software\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\Winlogon\\*" OR
 TargetObject="*\\Software\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\Image File Execution Options\\*" OR
 TargetObject="*\\Software\\Classes\\CLSID\\*" OR
 TargetObject="*\\System\\CurrentControlSet\\Services\\*")
(TargetObject="*\\Debugger" OR TargetObject="*\\InProcServer32" OR TargetObject="*\\ImagePath" OR TargetObject="*\\ServiceDll")
| rex field=Details "(?<ExecutablePath>C:\\\\[^\"]+\\.(exe|dll|ps1|vbs))"
| eval SuspiciousPath=if(like(Details, "%TEMP%") OR like(Details, "%APPDATA%") OR like(Details, "%Public%") OR like(Details, "%ProgramData%"), 1, 0)
| eval SuspiciousProcess=if(match(Image, "(?i)(cmd|powershell|wscript|cscript|mshta|rundll32)\.exe$"), 1, 0)
| eval RiskScore=SuspiciousPath*3 + SuspiciousProcess*2
| where RiskScore >= 3 OR NOT (Details LIKE "C:\\\\Program Files\\\\%" OR Details LIKE "C:\\\\Windows\\\\System32\\\\%")
| stats count, values(TargetObject) as RegistryKeys, values(Details) as Executables, values(Image) as ModifyingProcesses by Computer, User, _time
| where count > 0
| sort -_time
| table _time, Computer, User, RegistryKeys, Executables, ModifyingProcesses, count
| eval AlertSeverity=if(count > 3, "High", if(count > 1, "Medium", "Low"))

// Elastic Detection Rule - Registry Persistence
// Detection: Registry modification in autostart location by suspicious process
sequence by host.name, user.name with maxspan=5m
  [registry where
    (registry.path : (
      "*\\Software\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\Run*",
      "*\\Software\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\RunOnce*",
      "*\\Software\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\Winlogon\\*",
      "*\\Software\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\Image File Execution Options\\*",
      "*\\Software\\Classes\\CLSID\\*\\InProcServer32*",
      "*\\System\\CurrentControlSet\\Services\\*\\ImagePath"
    ) and
    registry.data.strings : ("*.exe", "*.dll", "*.ps1", "*.vbs", "*.bat", "*.cmd") and
    not registry.data.strings : (
      "C:\\\\Program Files\\\\*",
      "C:\\\\Program Files (x86)\\\\*",
      "C:\\\\Windows\\\\System32\\\\*",
      "C:\\\\Windows\\\\explorer.exe"
    )
  )]
  [process where
    process.parent.name : ("winword.exe", "excel.exe", "powerpnt.exe", "outlook.exe") and
    process.name : ("cmd.exe", "powershell.exe", "wscript.exe", "cscript.exe", "mshta.exe", "rundll32.exe")
  ]

// Detect registry persistence using Microsoft Defender for Endpoint telemetry
DeviceRegistryEvents
| where ActionType == "RegistryValueSet"
| where RegistryKey contains "Run" or
        RegistryKey contains "Winlogon" or
        RegistryKey contains "Image File Execution Options" or
        RegistryKey contains "CLSID" or
        RegistryKey contains @"\Services\"
| where RegistryValueData !startswith "C:\\\\Program Files\\\\" and
        RegistryValueData !startswith "C:\\\\Windows\\\\System32\\\\" and
        RegistryValueData !~ "explorer.exe"
// Join with process information
| join kind=inner (
    DeviceProcessEvents
    | where FileName in ("cmd.exe", "powershell.exe", "wscript.exe", "reg.exe", "mshta.exe")
    | project DeviceId, ProcessCreationTime=Timestamp, ProcessFileName=FileName, ProcessCommandLine=ProcessCommandLine, InitiatingProcessFileName
) on DeviceId
| where Timestamp between ((ProcessCreationTime - 2m) .. (ProcessCreationTime + 2m))
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, AccountName, RegistryKey, RegistryValueName, RegistryValueData, ProcessFileName, ProcessCommandLine, InitiatingProcessFileName
| order by Timestamp desc

event.code:13 AND winlog.event_data.TargetObject:(*\\Run* OR *\\RunOnce* OR *\\Winlogon\\* OR *\\Image\ File\ Execution\ Options\\* OR *\\CLSID\\*\\InProcServer32* OR *\\Services\\*\\ImagePath) AND NOT winlog.event_data.Details:("C:\\\\Program Files\\\\*" OR "C:\\\\Windows\\\\System32\\\\*")

tools.name: Sysmon
tools.usage: sysmon64.exe -accepteula -i sysmon-config.xml (see MIT-001 for comprehensive registry monitoring configuration)

tools.name: Autoruns
tools.usage: autoruns.exe (GUI) or autorunsc.exe -accepteula -a * -c -m -s (CLI with CSV output)

tools.name: Process Monitor (ProcMon)
tools.usage: Filter: Operation is RegSetValue, Path contains Run or Winlogon or Services

tools.name: RegShot
tools.usage: Take snapshot before/after suspected infection, compare to identify new persistence entries

tools.name: Microsoft Defender for Endpoint
tools.usage: Built-in detection for registry persistence via behavioral analytics and threat intelligence

compliance: ISO27001:2013 (A.12.4.1 - Event logging, A.9.2.3 - Management of privileged access rights)
NIST 800-53 Rev 5 (SI-4 - System Monitoring, AU-2 - Event Logging)
CIS Controls v8 (8.5 - Collect Detailed Audit Logs)

documentation: https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1547/001/ - MITRE ATT&CK: Registry Run Keys / Startup Folder
https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1546/015/ - MITRE ATT&CK: Component Object Model Hijacking
research_papers: https://www.hexacorn.com/blog/category/autostart-persistence/ - Hexacorn Autostart Persistence Research (140+ persistence locations)
https://www.elastic.co/security-labs/registry-persistence-detection - Elastic Security Labs: ML-based Registry Persistence Detection
tools: https://github.com/OTRF/ThreatHunter-Playbook - OTRF Threat Hunter Playbook (Registry Persistence Detections)
https://github.com/gentilkiwi/mimikatz - Mimikatz (for testing LSASS credential theft prevention)
